name: Oak Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Oak. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Oak surface cleaning without charring

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength for optimal absorption in Oak cellulose and lignin

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning resolution

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning with thermal management

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal without wood damage

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of contaminants

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete surface cleaning

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning pattern

dwellTime.description: Optimal dwell time per cleaning spot

power.description: Typical range for pulsed Nd:YAG lasers in wood cleaning to remove contaminants without charring; starting at 50 W for light soiling on oak, max limited by oak's low thermal conductivity (~0.17 W/m·K) to avoid substrate damage. Source: Studies on laser cleaning of wooden artifacts (e.g., Journal of Cultural Heritage, 2015).

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds for 1064 nm cleaning of oak; slower for heavy contamination (e.g., mold or grease) to ensure removal, faster for light dust; 500 mm/s optimal for balancing efficiency and heat control. Source: Industry manuals from IPG Photonics and Adapt Laser Systems.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for industrial cleaning of oak to minimize heat-affected zone (HAZ <50 μm); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precise but costlier applications; 20 ns balances ablation of contaminants with oak's sensitivity to thermal damage. Source: Laser physics research in Optics & Lasers in Engineering (2018).

repetition_rate.description: Typical for 1064 nm cleaning; 30 kHz recommended for oak to achieve fast coverage without excessive heat accumulation, as higher rates (>50 kHz) risk charring due to oak's low reflectivity (~10-20% at 1064 nm). Trade-off: higher freq speeds process but increases thermal load. Source: Academic literature, Applied Surface Science (2020).

energy_density.description: Fluence range for contaminant removal on wood; oak damage threshold ~3-5 J/cm² (ablation starts at ~1 J/cm² for organics); 1.5 J/cm² safe for cleaning without degrading cellulose structure. Conservative due to oak's melting/decomposition point ~200-300°C. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2017).

spot_size.description: Beam diameter for precise industrial cleaning; smaller for fine details on oak surfaces (e.g., engravings), larger for broad areas; 200 μm recommended for uniform contaminant removal without excessive overlap heating. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf lasers.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass for heavy layers; 2 passes typical for oak to ensure complete removal while monitoring for discoloration. Source: Industry case studies on wood restoration (e.g., Laser Cleaning Conference proceedings, 2019).

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage in scan patterns; 50% recommended for oak to avoid gaps in cleaning while preventing over-exposure and thermal buildup. Source: Process optimization in Surface and Coatings Technology (2021).
